methods.method_name: Seagrass and Epibiont Sample Collection and Preparation
methods.description: Process for collecting Zostera marina samples, fixing them, separating attached epifauna and epiphytic diatoms, measuring seagrass surface area, and preparing diatom frustules for microscopic analysis.

steps.description: Zostera marina was collected monthly from a seagrass meadow in Arikawa Bay, Nagasaki, Japan, from May 2017 to December 2018.
steps.searchable_summary: collect seagrass samples monthly from specified location

steps.description: One shoot of Z. marina (excluding the underground root) was carefully collected by snorkeling and placed inside a container with 1 L filtered seawater every month.
steps.searchable_summary: collect one seagrass shoot per month and place in container with filtered seawater
outputs: seagrass sample in container with seawater

steps.description: Samples were fixed with a mixed solution (2 : 1) of formalin (37% aqueous solution) and glutaraldehyde (25% aqueous solution) to achieve a final concentration of 1%.
steps.searchable_summary: fix samples with formalin and glutaraldehyde solution

steps.description: Any attached organisms were carefully removed manually with rubber gloves from each leaf of the preserved seagrass specimens.
steps.searchable_summary: manually remove attached organisms from seagrass leaves

steps.description: Individual plants were further washed with filtered seawater, which along with the removed organisms, were passed through a 300 µm net to separate the epifauna that remained on the net from the epiphytic diatoms.
steps.searchable_summary: wash seagrass and removed organisms through a 300 µm net to separate epifauna and diatoms

steps.description: The seagrass surface area was measured from digital images using ImageJ version 1.51, after taking a photograph of one side of the seagrass.
steps.searchable_summary: measure seagrass surface area from digital images using ImageJ

steps.description: The obtained value was doubled to obtain the total surface area.
steps.searchable_summary: calculate total seagrass surface area by doubling measured value

steps.description: The diatom frustules were treated following the procedure outlined by Nagumo (1995).
steps.searchable_summary: process diatom frustules according to Nagumo (1995) procedure

steps.description: The pre-concentrated diatom sample and distilled water were placed in a centrifuge tube with an equal amount of cleaning agent.
steps.searchable_summary: combine diatom sample, distilled water, and cleaning agent in centrifuge tube

steps.description: To enhance the effect of the cleaning agent, centrifuge tubes were placed in a 50°C water bath for approximately 30 min (Step 1).
steps.searchable_summary: incubate centrifuge tubes in 50°C water bath

steps.description: Centrifugation was performed to precipitate the diatom sample, and the supernatant was discarded (Step 2).
steps.searchable_summary: centrifuge sample to precipitate diatoms and discard supernatant

steps.description: Distilled water (10 mL) was added and the tubes rested for 10 min (Step 3).
steps.searchable_summary: add distilled water to diatom pellet and rest

steps.description: Steps 2 and 3 were repeated five times to remove the cleaning agent.
steps.searchable_summary: repeat centrifugation and washing steps five times

methods.description: Microscopic identification and counting of epiphytic diatoms and epifauna from prepared samples using various microscope types and reference literature.

steps.description: Aliquots (1 mL) were placed under an inverted light microscope (IX-70, Olympus, Tokyo, Japan) for counting and identifying epiphytic diatoms and epifauna.
steps.searchable_summary: count and identify diatoms and epifauna using inverted light microscopy
outputs: counts and identifications of diatoms and epifauna

steps.description: A second inverted microscope (BX-51, Olympus, Tokyo, Japan) and a scanning electron microscope (JSM-6390LAKII, JEOL, Tokyo, Japan) were used to aid in the identification of diatoms.
steps.searchable_summary: use additional microscopes (inverted and SEM) to aid diatom identification

steps.description: A stereoscopic microscope (ST30RDL, AS ONE, Tokyo, Japan) was used to closely examine the epifauna where necessary.
steps.searchable_summary: use stereoscopic microscope to examine epifauna

steps.description: The following literature was utilized to identify diatoms and epifauna specimens: Diatoms: Biology and morphology of the genera (Round et al. 2007), H. Kobayashi s atlas of Japanese diatoms based on electron microscopy (Kobayashi et al. 2006), An illustrated guide to marine plankton in Japan (Chihara & Murano 1996), and Benthos of the tidal flat (Suzuki et al. 2013).
steps.searchable_summary: use specified literature for identification of diatoms and epifauna

methods.description: Statistical analysis of time-series data for epiphytic diatom density, epifauna density, and Zostera marina leaf area using Generalized Additive Models (GAM) and Generalized Linear Models (GLM) in R, fitted using Bayesian methods.

steps.description: All statistical analyses were performed using R (R Core Team 2020).
steps.searchable_summary: perform statistical analysis using R software

steps.description: The epiphytic diatom total density, epifauna total density, and Z. marina leaf area time-series were examined independently using a GAM that applied a gamma distribution and log link function to the observations.
steps.searchable_summary: fit Generalized Additive Model (GAM) with gamma distribution and log link to time-series data
inputs: time-series data (diatom total density, epifauna total density, leaf area)

steps.description: A generalized linear model (GLM) with the same distributional parameters of the GAM was fitted to each time series to serve as a null model.
steps.searchable_summary: fit Generalized Linear Model (GLM) as a null model with same distribution/link as GAM

steps.description: The smoothing functions used in the GAM was a p-spline with 15 basis functions (Eilers and Marx 1996).
steps.searchable_summary: specify p-spline smoothing function with 15 basis functions for GAM

steps.description: Each epiphytic diatom type and epifauna group were examined using a similar model; however, the p-spline was constructed with eight basis functions, the observations were transformed using the natural logarithm, and the distribution was assumed to be Gaussian.
steps.searchable_summary: fit GAM with p-spline (8 basis functions), natural log transformation, and Gaussian distribution for individual types/groups
inputs: time-series data (individual diatom types, epifauna groups)
outputs: fitted GAM models for individual types/groups

steps.description: A GLM was again used as the null model.
steps.searchable_summary: fit GLM as null model for individual types/groups
outputs: fitted GLM null models for individual types/groups

steps.description: The fitting was performed using Bayesian methods with the rstanarm (Goodrich et al. 2020) package, which uses Hamiltonian Markov Chains (HMC) to explore the posterior distribution.
steps.searchable_summary: fit models using Bayesian methods with rstanarm package (Hamiltonian Markov Chains)

steps.description: Weakly informative priors were applied to the intercept (i.e., a normal distribution with a location of 0 and a scale of 2), the coefficients of the smoothing functions (i.e., an exponential distribution with a location of 2), and the scale of the gamma distribution (i.e., an exponential distribution with a location of 0.5).
steps.searchable_summary: apply weakly informative priors for Bayesian model fitting

steps.description: A total of four chains were run for 6000 iterations each.
steps.searchable_summary: run Bayesian model fitting with 4 chains and 6000 iterations per chain

steps.description: Convergence, HMC mixing, and posterior validity were assessed visually.
steps.searchable_summary: visually assess convergence, mixing, and validity of Bayesian model chains
outputs: assessment of model convergence and validity

steps.description: The two competing models were compared using the difference in the expected log pointwise predictive density of the leave-one-out crossvalidation estimate, elpd loo (Vehtari et al. 2017).
steps.searchable_summary: compare competing models using elpd_loo (expected log pointwise predictive density)
